I_kwDOJOvPRM6vaeLX.title: Build a Simple Telegram DM Automation Tool
I_kwDOJOvPRM6vaeLX.body:
### Time Estimate

- [x] I have added a time estimate label

### Background Information

nReach was too expensive
but we still need to send telegram DMs to large swaths of people

## Core Requirements

-  Written in TypeScript for maintainability and team support
-  Runs locally as a server that can receive webhook requests
-  Configurable to use the "Ana2" business Telegram account
-  Sends Telegram direct messages to specified recipients
-  Updates Airtable cells with status ("Sent" or "Failed")
-  Triggered by Airtable automation when a cell is changed to "send!"

## Technical Implementation

-  HTTP server that listens for POST requests from Airtable
-  Telegram client implementation that uses user account (not a bot)
-  Airtable integration that can parse cell URLs and update statuses
-  Environment variable configuration for credentials and settings
-  Proper error handling and logging

## Deliverables

- TypeScript source code in a GitHub repository
- Configuration files (.env.example, tsconfig.json, package.json)
- Documentation (README.md) with setup and usage instructions
- Airtable script for the automation trigger

## Success Criteria

The task is considered complete when:

1. The local server is running and can receive webhook requests
2. The server successfully sends a Telegram DM when triggered
3. The specified Airtable cell is updated with "Sent" or "Failed" status
4. The entire flow works end-to-end: Airtable trigger → Server → Telegram DM → Airtable update

## Out of Scope

- Hosting the server in production
- Authentication and security measures
- Scaling for high volume
- User interface or dashboard
- Multiple account management

These completion criteria align with the business requirement for an automated system that can send Telegram DMs and update Airtable cells, while keeping the implementation simple and focused on the core functionality.

[^1]: 2025 Marketing Narrative: 
Our core offering is straightforward: performance analytics for your software engineering team. While GitHub provides surface-level metrics like commit counts and pull requests, UbiquityOS dives deeper, delivering AI-powered qualitative insights that measure the actual impact of every contribution. Instead of raw numbers, you get meaningful performance data that highlights each developer’s strengths, identifies skill gaps, and lays the groundwork for a stronger, data-driven engineering management.
But our platform goes far beyond analytics. UbiquityOS acts as an AI-powered manager, streamlining your daily administrative management with intelligent task-talent matchmaking, automated proposal submissions, and transparent payment mechanisms, including integrated payment cards for cashing out rewards, to support teams from start to finish. Developers can self-assign tasks, receive AI-driven assistance with full repo context, and stay organized with automated issue deduplication—ensuring nothing slips through the cracks. For teams that prefer immediate and flexible payments, UbiquityOS integrates seamlessly with Ubiquity virtual payment cards, enabling real-time payouts upon task completion. Everything is configurable, so whether you need just performance analytics or a fully automated AI development manager, UbiquityOS adapts to fit your workflow—putting you in control every step of the way.

- dashboard specs below:

## Ubiquity OS — Frontend Dashboard Spec (GitHub UI Extension)

This spec focuses solely on the frontend dashboard interface for Ubiquity OS.  "XP" would simply replace the existing "WXDAI" units.

### Goal

Deliver a lightweight but powerful GitHub-native dashboard that makes developer contribution data visible, gamified, and actionable. The dashboard supports both developers and managers by surfacing metrics around impact, engagement, and performance.

### Dashboard Locations

1. **Organization-Level Dashboard** (`/orgs/[org]/xp-dashboard`)
2. **Repository-Level Dashboard** (`/repos/[org]/[repo]/xp-dashboard`)
3. **Contributor Profile Overlay** (opens on contributor click)

---

### Overview Panel

This is the main landing view for both org- and repo-level dashboards. It summarizes contribution activity over a selectable time range.

Includes:
- Total XP (timeframe + all-time)
- XP by category (Coding, Reviewing, Spec Writing, Comments)
- Leaderboard: top contributors by XP
- Velocity trend chart: XP earned over time
- Engagement overview: active contributors, PRs reviewed, comments made

### XP Categories

Each activity maps to one of several standardized XP categories:

| Category             | Description                                      | XP Source                                     |
|----------------------|--------------------------------------------------|-----------------------------------------------|
| Spec Writing         | Issues authored with spec/design labels         | XP for creating and content quality           |
| Coding               | Merged PRs where user is assignee               | XP based on issue priority + estimated time   |
| Reviewing            | PR reviews, approvals, change requests          | XP per review and comment weight              |
| Commenting           | Comments on issues or PRs                       | XP for helpful or upvoted comments            |

### Contributor View

Drill-down modal or side panel for individual contributors.

Includes:
- Total XP (timeframe + all-time)
- XP by type (bar or donut chart)
- Weekly XP trendline
- Recent contributions (linked to GitHub)
- Most active repos or categories
- Optional badge/streak system for consistency

### Admin & Config Panel (Managers Only)

A separate settings interface available via external dashboard or modal:
- Adjust XP weight by category
- Configure labels for categories (e.g., what counts as "Spec")
- Set XP-to-bounty payout ratio (if applicable)
- Export XP data (CSV or JSON)
- Connect external bounty boards or task managers

### Repo-Level Analytics

When viewing a specific repository, the dashboard shows:
- XP earned within this repo
- Contributor leaderboard scoped to this repo
- Active XP categories in this repo
- Weekly velocity of contributions

### Design Considerations

- Fully styled to match GitHub’s native light/dark mode
- Minimal, readable typography using GitHub UI conventions
- Smooth animated transitions on XP updates and charts
- Color-coded tags and charts for fast visual scanning
- Real-time updates powered by GitHub webhooks

### Filters and Controls

- Date range selector (Last 7/30/90 days, custom)
- Repo selector (at org level)
- Sortable tables by XP, name, last activity
- Category toggles for filtering views
- Export data (button in bottom-right corner)
- "Open Full Dashboard" for expanded analytics (external app link)

---

### Relevant Links

[simple prototype](https://airtable.com/app11xYKIG5B3YyFE/shrtcXqqHWPETXNcD)

While GitHub provides surface-level metrics like commit counts and pull requests, UbiquityOS dives deeper, delivering AI-powered qualitative insights that measure the actual impact of every contribution. Instead of raw numbers, you get meaningful performance data that highlights each developer’s strengths, identifies skill gaps, and lays the groundwork for a stronger, data-driven engineering management.

> But our platform goes far beyond analytics. UbiquityOS acts as an AI-powered manager, streamlining your daily administrative management with intelligent task-talent matchmaking, automated proposal submissions, and transparent payment mechanisms, including integrated payment cards for cashing out rewards, to support teams from start to finish. Developers can self-assign tasks, receive AI-driven assistance with full repo context, and stay organized with automated issue deduplication—ensuring nothing slips through the cracks. For teams that prefer immediate and flexible payments, UbiquityOS integrates seamlessly with Ubiquity virtual payment cards, enabling real-time payouts upon task completion. Everything is configurable, so whether you need just performance analytics or a fully automated AI development manager, UbiquityOS adapts to fit your workflow—putting you in control every step of the way.

I_kwDOJOvPRM6b0-Dt.title: Virtual cards provider
I_kwDOJOvPRM6b0-Dt.body:
https://www.reloadly.com has some drawbacks:
1. Upper limit of a single virtual gift card (1000 USD)
2. Not scalable in terms that if we want a single contributor to have a single virtual card (and brand it as "ubiquity card") then right now we have to mint a dozens of gift cards instead of a single virtual bank card.

We should find a proper virtual cards provider with the following features (available via API):
1. Create a virtual card for a contributor
2. Top up a virtual card of a contributor
3. Check virtual card balance
4. Virtual cards must be tokenized (i.e. can be added to Apple/Google wallet) 
5. Virtual cards can be used in South Korea, USA and EU countries

I've sent a couple of inquiries:
| Service  | Status |
| ------------- | ------------- |
| https://www.ininal.com  | Asked for details  |
| https://apibank.club  | Asked for details, doesn't support South Korea  |
| https://wanttopay.net  | No reply  |
| https://pst.net  | Asked for details, doesn't support Apple/Google wallets  |

### January 2025 Addendum

It seems that we are having reliability concerns with reloadly. 

As long as we can find a partner with no monthly fees then it can serve well for our early testing and research of the Ubiquity card program. 

Deliverable should be something similar to the "solution costs" section of our [2023 report](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1m3fcKkPg7aIhkLD0vfJxTZFwGBUdq1vv6UhL2Jt9gcc/edit?usp=sharing). Basically find out pricing and terms for similar providers.

I_kwDOJOvPRM6pI47n.title: Develop Marketing Narrative 2025
I_kwDOJOvPRM6pI47n.body:
### Time Estimate

- [x] I have added a time estimate label

### Background Information

I know that a narrative is arguably more important than the product itself in our industry. 

An example is when we were fundraising for our stablecoin, we spent a couple years building an economically sound and secure stablecoin but didn't catch any interest until one day we decided to call it "the stablecoin for crypto gaming" while there was a crypto gaming hype cycle. We were able to immediately secure the $2M USD seed afterwards. 

AI is hot these days and I think that we should spend some time to craft a compelling narrative and reflect this in all of our public materials. 

---

One narrative direction that I've been thinking on for a bit is "UbiquityOS is an internet of AI agents for completing work." We have all the building blocks for this.

Basically the general idea is that due to the clear financial incentives in our system, developers are incentivized to automate task completion to collect the most money. The best AI agents will collect the most money. Eventually as the market gets more competitive, I imagine that the cost for completing tasks will dramatically lower. 

I think we can craft a vision of some utopian future that is the result of UbiquityOS. 

### Completion Criteria

- We should collaborate on a Google Doc to craft a compelling narrative that can be used across all of our public materials. 

### Relevant Links

https://docs.google.com/document/d/1r6bm30i_Heb8w_D3HPOapVtKGdWIhvajt1kS_792Xxs/edit

I_kwDOJOvPRM6ZkWA_.title: Update uAD -> UUSD on Etherscan
I_kwDOJOvPRM6ZkWA_.body:
The Etherscan customer support team is incompetent. Get this symbol change done. `uAD` -> `UUSD`



### Email Response

> Hi Chad McCaldwell,
> 
> We have received your submission for the token information update. However, at this point, we are unable to update the token’s page due to the lack of some information, or differences therein provided, required for our perusal.
> 
> For your reference, these are the most common reasons why an update request could not be processed:
> The token’s official website is not accessible/safe to visit
> There is no clear information available about the token/project
> Some of the placeholders on the websites are not updated (images, links, etc.)
> Some of the links provided are broken
> The project does not provide transparent/clear information on its founder and team members (with supporting public professional profiles i.e LinkedIn profile or equivalent)
> The sender’s email address is not the same as the token/project’s official domain
> The information provided is false/misrepresentation of public entities or other projects
> The token name and/or symbol may be susceptible to brand infringement
> 
> You may re-submit your update request once you have rectified the issues. It is also recommended to provide as much information as possible, (i.e. team member profiles, whitepaper, project’s official channels, etc) to support your update request. When in doubt, we recommend checking our token update guidelines here: https://info.etherscan.com/how-to-update-token-info/ (the guideline was published on Etherscan but applies to [all explorers](https://etherscan.io/eaas) developed by Etherscan).
> 
> *Kindly take note that although we are unable to update the token’s page, all pertinent information about the token, such as transactions, token supply, token holders, etc, are still publicly available for anyone to interact with. 
> 
> Best regards,
> - Team Etherscan.io [Nabilah J]

I_kwDOJOvPRM6lgL6U.title: Educational Materials
I_kwDOJOvPRM6lgL6U.body:
We should develop educational materials that showcase the system’s features through multimedia elements such as photographs, videos, and a blog-style narrative.

These materials should be integrated into our marketing and sales strategies to effectively communicate the advantages of UbiquityOS.


Given the amount of features that we have, it might make sense to prioritize features that may be more "sexy" and stream out the content over time.

This first task can highlight our AI related features such as:

- talent recommendation 
- issue deduplication 
- conversation scoring
- `/ask`

I also think that we should explain the core architecture of our system and how everything revolves around the concept of "tasks" with associated priority and time labels.  

Each post should include images and possibly even a voice over video if it makes sense to. 

These can be delivered on our notion at dao.ubq.fi

I_kwDOJOvPRM6pmngT.title: Demo Dashboard + `ubiquity-os-simulant`
I_kwDOJOvPRM6pmngT.body:
### Mockup 

![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/550c09cf-0747-452b-a24d-f4e074479f5a)

### Overview

We should have a "try demo" button on ubq.fi and this should bring the user to a "Start Demo UI" to be able to easily set up:

1. "Login with GitHub" to create a new demo repository in their GitHub[^1^]. 
2. Install `ubiquity-os[bot]` - our production deployment.
3. Create the `.ubiquity-os` repository and push `.ubiquity-os.config.yml` with default settings (hardcoded evm wallet with no money should be fine.)
4. Add @ubiquity-os-simulant - a fake GitHub user profile with a personal access token that we can control with a GitHub Action PER plugin demo[^3^] that runs on timers i.e. ~5 minutes with manual delays per action taken (i.e. leave a comment, open a pull request, file an issue) to simulate a team member that interacts with UbiquityOS. 

The "try demo" dashboard should have buttons for each of [these capabilities](https://github.com/ubiquity/business-development/issues/109#issuecomment-2579691293) that trigger the @ubiquity-os-simulant to run choreographed scenarios to demonstrate each capability. It should basically pretend to be the user's knowledgeable team member. 

Capabilities shortlist:
- Payouts
- `/ask`
- Task Matchmaking (I hope we don't pollute our vector embeddings data with these demos)


### Research Needed

- We need a webhook receiver inside the simulant that listens to requests from the "Start Demo UI"
   - It probably makes sense to just use a modified kernel[^2^], because it needs to invoke the "choreographed scenario plugins" 
- I realized that some events, like being assigned, requires a normal user profile and can't be done with a bot account, so we will need to make a fake user profile and control it with a personal access token. https://chatgpt.com/share/67ab5c0c-fc58-8001-87e1-cfab828d5819


### Deliverables

- [x] "Start Demo UI" - Forked from https://github.com/ubiquity/onboard.ubq.fi
- [x] @ubiquity-os-simulant user profile, and personal access token creation with correct permissions, and its webhook receiver code.
- [x] One choreographed scenario GitHub Action. For now, we can do a simple hello world that creates a new repository, opens a new issue, and calls the `/help` menu

### Next Steps

- We need to spec out the choreography of each scenario. 
- We also need to decide on one to get ready for the conference next week. 

[^1^]: Does it make sense to just file demo issues inside of the `.ubiquity-os` repository? 
[^2^]: It needs to be able to load its own config most likely i.e. `.ubiquity-os-simulant.config.yml`
[^3^]: Or we can do separate typescript files/directories per demo, whatever makes more sense I'm not sure. But separate GitHub Actions may make changing any environment variables simpler.

I_kwDOJOvPRM6pn0mI.title: Base Pay Shift
I_kwDOJOvPRM6pn0mI.body:
### Objective:
Analyze current compensation patterns and design an optimized incentive structure to reduce burn rate while ensuring high performers maintain or increase total compensation as base pay decreases.

### Requirements:

1. Data Analysis:
   - Track and analyze on-chain transactions for identified team wallets
   - Compare base pay vs incentive-based earnings for each team member
   - Calculate current burn rate and project future scenarios

2. Compensation Structure Design:
   - Plan quarterly base pay reduction steps (-$2k each)
   - Simulate incentive mechanisms (we have a tool for this) to project incentives based earnings. 
   - Target maximum 4 reduction phases from current max ($10k) to minimum/zero base pay

### Key Parameters:
- Maximum base pay: $10k
- Reduction increment: $2k per phase
- Implementation frequency: Quarterly maximum
- Total phases: Up to 4
- For this task only focus on the first reduction.

### Available Data:
- Team wallet addresses with associated identities
- Historical base pay records
- On-chain transaction history
- Performance metrics and incentive payments

### Deliverables:
1. Current state analysis of compensation distribution
2. Projected impact analysis of base pay reductions
3. Proposed incentive structure adjustments
4. Implementation timeline and phase triggers

### Success Criteria:
- Reduced overall burn rate
- High performers maintain or increase total compensation

Note: This is a one-time analysis project with specific implementation phases, not requiring ongoing maintenance.

I_kwDOJOvPRM6oDdfb.title: Post Rick Onboarding Call
I_kwDOJOvPRM6oDdfb.body:
This is a ChatGPT generated summary of the [meeting](https://fathom.video/calls/220187334?tab=summary). 

Will need to break down into separate issues. 

## Priority Shipping Based on the Conversation

1. Refocus on Core Value Proposition (High Priority)
	•	Shift focus from pricing/marketplace features to project management and efficiency tracking.
	•	Rebrand positioning to appeal to broader tech organizations beyond Web3.
	•	Develop a clear Unique Selling Proposition (USP) that differentiates from existing AI-powered project management tools.

2. Task Completion & Time Tracking (High Priority)
	•	Implement start and stop timestamps for task tracking:
	•	When an issue is assigned, record a timestamp.
	•	When a PR closes the issue, calculate total work time.
	•	Compare time estimates vs. actual completion time.
	•	Track developer work habits and efficiency over time.
	•	Generate reports for managers to see how accurately time estimates align with execution.

3. AI-Driven Task Recommendation System (High Priority)
	•	Improve vector embeddings to automatically assign relevant tasks based on historical work.
	•	Display task recommendations on GitHub issues with >80% relevance.
	•	Make task assignments more dynamic, allowing managers to see which developer is best suited for a task.

4. Automate Prioritization & Kanban Sorting (Medium Priority)
	•	Automatically sort tasks in GitHub project boards based on priority labels.
	•	Ensure high-priority issues don’t get buried in multiple pages.
	•	Provide dashboard insights showing what’s most urgent and who is assigned to what.

5. Launch Customer Discovery & Validation (High Priority)
	•	Conduct 10+ customer interviews in the next 2 weeks with tech orgs (outside Web3) to refine feature set.
	•	Ask: Would they pay for automated efficiency tracking?
	•	Identify common pain points in software project management that overlap across companies.

6. Create a New Landing Page & Demo (High Priority)
	•	Remove all crypto/Web3 references.
	•	Focus messaging on:
	•	How it helps project managers track efficiency.
	•	How it improves team prioritization and accountability.
	•	How it offers AI-driven insights into engineering workflows.
	•	Add a waitlist button and simple form:
	•	“Company Name”
	•	“Role”
	•	“Biggest challenge in managing development teams”
	•	Share the landing page with GitHub Universe contacts, OpenQ, and broader tech communities.

7. Remove Marketplace-Dependent Features & Test Core Plugins (Medium Priority)
	•	Set up mock repositories that exclude pricing/marketplace features.
	•	Test standalone plugins for efficiency tracking, prioritization, and task recommendations.
	•	Measure whether these features still deliver strong value.

8. Ensure Recommendations Work With Limited Data (Medium Priority)
	•	Improve logic so recommendations work even with a small number of completed issues.
	•	Allow recommendations based on job titles, GitHub contributions, and past assignments.

Next Steps
	1.	Set a 2-week goal to interview 10+ tech orgs and refine core features based on common feedback.
	2.	Launch the new landing page and waitlist ASAP.
	3.	Ship the task tracking & efficiency reports ASAP, as Riccardo expressed willingness to pay for this today.
	4.	Test AI task recommendations and priority automation, improving GitHub integration.
	5.	Remove crypto dependencies in key plugins and validate their standalone value.
	6.	Schedule follow-up call with OpenQ in a few weeks to show progress on the recommendations and efficiency tracking.

TL;DR – What to Prioritize?
	•	Efficiency tracking (time estimates vs. actuals).
	•	AI task recommendations (based on past work).
	•	Automatic prioritization & sorting (so high-priority tasks surface first).
	•	Customer interviews (to validate non-crypto use cases).
	•	Landing page & demo (to gather real-world interest).

This aligns directly with what Riccardo was most excited about and has the highest potential for market traction.

I_kwDOJOvPRM6ZiybP.title: M12 Ventures for GitHub - Investee Relationship Building
I_kwDOJOvPRM6ZiybP.body: ## Context > We’re delighted to highlight the first startup that has received our first investment out of the M12 GitHub Fund. Congratulations to [CodeSee](https://www.codesee.io/), an open source platform that enables developer teams to visually understand their codebases. Codesee is equipping developers with the best tools to unleash their capabilities and productivity and is shaping the way software developers work on their world-changing projects. This is central to why the M12 GitHub Fund was founded. [Source](https://m12.vc/news/announcing-the-m12-github-fund/). I looked up the founders, [CEO](https://www.linkedin.com/in/shaneak/), [CTO](https://www.linkedin.com/in/joshleven/) and I was inspired for a new idea: we should try and develop relationships with M12 investees. We can probably get introductions and advice through them. ## Investees > ByteWax > Python framework that enables you to build streaming data pipelines and real-time apps. > > > CodeSee > Cloud-based code visualization software that allows you to visualize, review, automate, and collaborate on your code. > > > Guardrails > Open source Python package for specifying structure and type and validating and correcting the outputs of large language models (LLMs). > > > Mermaid Chart > Text-based diagramming and charting tool that enables you to create and modify diagrams dynamically, built on top of the open source tool, Mermaid. > > > Nixtla > Open source library for building time series forecasting models and a proprietary time series LLM. > > > Novu > Open source notification infrastructure platform that provides a single API to manage all communication channels from one place. > > > Rubbrband > Provides a Python API to automatically evaluate AI-generated images from Stable Diffusion, SDXL, Midjourney, or DALLE-3. > > > OpenSauced > A platform that identifies the best developers and engineers by their code contributions and trending open source projects. > > > Tooljet > Low-code platform for building business applications using a drag-and-drop application builder. > > > Unify AI > Open source tool that unifies ML framework, infrastructure, hardware, and libraries to maximize the portability of ML codebases with one line of code. - [GitHub Source](https://resources.github.com/github-fund/). - [M12 Source](https://m12.vc/github-fund/). ## Additional Thoughts Slightly off topic but I'm more bullish on working with M12 because they seemingly recently added web3. Looks like we hit 66% of all their investment criteria. And then of course they made their dedicated GitHub fund which we also hit (so now 72%) its quite close to a perfect match. - [x] [AI](https://m12.vc/focus/ai/) - [ ] [Cloud infrastructure](https://m12.vc/focus/cloud-infrastructure/) - [ ] [Cybersecurity](https://m12.vc/focus/cybersecurity/) - [x] [Developer tools](https://m12.vc/focus/developer-tools/) - [x] [Vertical SaaS](https://m12.vc/focus/vertical-saas/) - [x] [Web3 + gaming](https://m12.vc/focus/web3-gaming/)[^1^] We could expect to get up to a $1M USD check from them, but unfortunately they don't generally lead rounds according to the GitHub source FAQ > Is the investment dilutive? > It is an equity investment. We typically do not lead investment rounds. For non-dilutive funding, please check out [GitHub Accelerator](https://accelerator.github.com/) or [GitHub Sponsors](https://github.com/sponsors). [^1^]: A funny name for an "AI + Blockchain" investee: https://fortune.com/crypto/2024/08/27/exclusive-microsoft-space-and-time-framework-lightspeed-arrington-ai-crypto/

I_kwDOMPDHHs7VozgP.title: Universal SDK for WUSIWUG editors
I_kwDOMPDHHs7VozgP.body:
Lucia Browser SDK – Integration Spec (Wix / WordPress / Shopify / Framer)

🎯 Objective

Enable non-technical users (e.g. IG influencers, small merchants) to install Lucia SDK via a simple script embed, enabling automatic tracking of page views, button clicks, and scrolls.

🧩 Core Requirements

1. Universal Embed Script

  • Provide a single <script> tag to paste into Wix / WordPress / Shopify / Framer settings.

  • Example:

    

<script src="https://cdn.luciaprotocol.com/lucia-sdk-latest.min.js"></script>
<script>
  LuciaSDK.init({
    // API Key from https://ads.clickinsights.xyz
    api_key: 'bbbbbbbb-a7298818-3265d018-e9a648ae-95371b44-d25e6c77-38069b79-1e4bd263'
  });

  LuciaSDK.pageView('PAGE-NAME');
</script>

  • SDK auto-initializes via LuciaSDK.init()

2. Auto Page Tracking

  • SDK automatically triggers:

    LuciaSDK.pageView('projects')

  • Should debounce duplicate triggers (e.g., single-page app reloads or Wix internal router updates).

3. Button Click Tracking

  • Auto-attach click listeners to all visible <button> and <a> elements.

  • On click:

    LuciaSDK.buttonClick({
  ...
});

4. Scroll Tracking

  • Emit event when user scrolls past key thresholds (5%,10%,15%, 20%, 25%, 50%, 75%, 100%):

    LuciaSDK.trackScroll({ event: 'scroll', value: scrollDepth });

5. Attribution Context

Every event includes:

{
  "utm": getUtmParams(),
  "device": getBrowserData().device,
  "screen": getBrowserData().screen,
  "browser": getBrowserData().browser
}

6. CMS-Specific Implementation Notes

Platform | Implementation Path | Notes -- | -- | -- Wix | Paste JS into Site Settings → Tracking & Analytics (Runs on all pages). | Fallback: per-page embed if global not available. WordPress | Add script via Header/Footer plugin or functions.php. | Compatible with both Classic & Gutenberg editors. Shopify | Add to theme.liquid before . | Use window.ShopifyAnalytics.meta to enrich attribution data. Framer | Paste via Custom Code → Footer Script. | SPA-friendly — detect route changes via window.onpopstate.

User Story 📸

As an IG influencer with a Wix site,
I want to install Lucia SDK by pasting one script,
So I can see which buttons and pages my visitors interact with
— without needing to code or configure tracking manually.

User Story ⛴️

As an e-commerce store owner with a Shopify site, among other sites like Amazon
I want to install Lucia SDK by pasting one script,
So I can track conversion rate on major buttons for purchasing, track conversion rates on other minor buttons like contact form fill outs, and pages my visitors interact with
— without needing to code extensively or configure tracking page by page.

✅ Deliverables

  • CDN-ready build sdk.min.js

  • Cross-platform event tracking (page, click, scroll)

  • Docs examples for Wix, WordPress, Shopify, Framer

  • Event schema alignment with backend fingerprints table

I_kwDOMPDHHs7OY3nx.title: Passively Collect All Emails
I_kwDOMPDHHs7OY3nx.body:
# Detect “email-ish” fields with a scored heuristic + runtime signals. 

input[type=email]

input[type=text|search], textarea whose name/id/class include: email, e-mail, mail, login, user, username, handle, contact, work_email, business_email

autocomplete hints: email, username

Associated <label for=...> or ARIA text containing: email, e-mail, work email, business email, school email, username, handle

pattern attributes that include @ or typical email regex fragments

placeholder text with @ or “email”, “username”

### Steps Summarized

1. Detect likely email fields (reuse your detector).
2. Attach listeners (change, blur, submit, paste).
3. Validate & normalize the value (RFC-5322-ish regex + trim/lowercase).
4. Respect consent/config (don’t capture if disallowed).
5. Queue and send to API backend

# Scoring

Score each field with additive weighted rules to produce a confidence score (0–1).

+5 if type=email

+4 if autocomplete=email

+3 if name|id|class matches /\b(e-?mail|mail)\b/i

+2 if label/placeholder contains “email” or includes @

+1 if pattern contains @ or TLDs (.com, .edu, etc.)

−2 if label contains just “username” (track separately as username-ish)

−3 if field is hidden/readonly/offscreen

# False Positive Control

Keep email vs username as separate labels. Some apps accept both in one box; that’s fine—expose both labels with confidence.
